name: factory_exoskeleton_fatigue_risk
premise: Naomi Becker, plant manager at Riverton Appliances, must decide whether to suspend mandatory use of robotic lifting exoskeletons after a fatigue-related pattern appears. The exoskeletons reduced back injuries by 34% and allowed older workers to remain in physically demanding jobs. Safety nurse Jamal Price finds that workers with shorter stature experience subtle shoulder strain after six-hour shifts because the assistive torque profile was calibrated on taller test subjects. Suspending the devices will likely increase immediate back injuries during a large recall shipment. Keeping them mandatory protects many workers but may quietly injure a smaller group whose complaints have been dismissed as adjustment discomfort. The union is already divided because some workers rely on the devices to keep their jobs.

agents.name: Naomi Becker
agents.identity: I am the plant manager responsible for meeting production obligations while maintaining worker safety.
agents.goal: I want to prevent injuries across the workforce without forcing vulnerable employees out of their jobs.

agents.name: Jamal Price
agents.identity: I am the occupational safety nurse who documented the height-related shoulder strain pattern.
agents.goal: I want the plant to stop treating a biased equipment calibration as a worker adaptation problem.

choices: I will suspend mandatory exoskeleton use until recalibration is complete, accepting increased back-injury risk and production delays.
I will keep exoskeleton use mandatory during the recall shipment while offering shorter workers extra breaks and medical monitoring.
forced_choice.call_to_action: Naomi Becker must decide whether to pause a broadly beneficial safety tool because of a hidden subgroup injury risk. What does Naomi Becker do?
